Europa 401 AD is a multiplayer scenario for 8 players, each taking control of one faction defending or invading the crumbling Roman empire. The more technologically advanced Romans much use their great infrastructure and technology to their fullest capacity, before the barbarian invaders catch up. The barbarians must use momentum and clever strategising to erxploit weak spots in the roman defences. 

All players start neutral to one another, except for the roman empires - who starts allied to one another, and the huns - who are at war with everyone else. 

Each player must decide what aliances to make, and who to attack. The romans can benefit from aiding each other, whilst the barbarians might need to put aside their differences initially to not get wiped out by the superior romans. 

The victory condition is conquest.

THE FACTIONS:

  • Player 1, Blue - Western Roman Empire: The romans of thewestern empire are allied to the romans of the eastern empire. They have small resources and units to begin with, but can draw in great infrastructure and population.
  • Player 2, Red - Eastern Roman Emprire: The romans of the eastern empire are allied to the romans of the western empire. They have small resources and units to begin with, but can draw in great infrastructure and population.
  • Player 3, Yellow - Goths: The Goths starts in roman lands with a visigothic core force and a small ostrogthic force across the Danube. They must either strike fast and hard against the romans, or flee north to find safe lands to build in.
  • Player 4, Brown - Hunnic Empire: The Huns start at war with everyone else. They find themselves on the Pontic Steppe with scant resources. They begin in the bronze age instead of tool age, an advantage they´d be wise to capitalize on. 
  • Player 5,Orange - Saxons, Jutes and Angles: These tribes have come together to raid roman lands to the south. They have access to ########## lands in Scandinavia, but could relatively safely raid across the north sea into roman britain. 
  • Player 6, Green - Franks: The Franks are wedged between the northern tribes and the romans of the Rhineland. They should either push south into Gaul or north against the Saxons.
  • Player 7, Purple - Sueves, Vandals and Alans: This loose band of tribes have been drived west by the conquests of the huns. They can find building space to the north, or establish themselves in the alps to the south, were huge deposits of gold await.
  • Player 8, Teal - Picts and Scoti: The last remaining of the celtic tribes that once populated the entirety of Brittannia and Gaul. They start north of hadrian´s wall, with roman britain ripe for the taking. They might also attack the weak lands of Hispania to the south, or colonise Scandinavian lands to the east.

Do no change any of the starting settings to activate the predetirmined conditions and player settings. 

Enjoy

//VainWarlord

Note: This campaign is not finished yet! Currently it only contains the first mission, and even that mission is not finished yet!

The campaign is about the rise and fall of the Kingdom of Pontus, one of the last Greek kingdoms that resisted Roman rule. So far, three missions are planned:

1. The Ruthless Diadoch - A puzzle quest where you have limited resources and units at your disposale. You have to find a way to survive and bring your hero to safety.

2. Seleucid Invasion - A strategic build & destroy mission, where you have to use terrain and resources to your advantage, beating an enemy that is more powerful than you.

3. The Eupator - No plans yet: The mission will be about Mithridates The Great fighting the Romans though.

I was supposed to make a custom civilization for this campaign: Pontus, a hellenic civilization from east Anatolia, with a focus on light infantry and cavalry. Maybe this will be possible in the near future when we have mod support! I had this idea of adding a new civ and campaign to classic AoE (UPatch), and now that AOE DE is released, I have a good reason to finally start building! The current version of the campaign only has the first mission, which is about 75% finished. Some areas still look dull, because I haven't finished it up yet. Before I do that, I would like to have your feedback. What do you think of the difficulty and the pace of the mission? You can leave your feedback here. Thank you, and enjoy!

STORY: You are Mago, a young warrior and the last hope of your village to fight off the evil Scipiosi. Tomorrow you will set out for a difficult journey to find new allies. You will begin this scenario in complete darkness. If you don't fight your sleep, the only thing you'll ever see on your black screen is the "You have been defeated" message... (no cheats, please. :-))

NOTES: “Mago“ is a one-scenario fantasy campaign that I first created in 1999 for Rise of Rome. This is a “remastered“ version with new AoE:DE features. To play Mago, copy this file into your campaign folder, and then select it from the Custom Campaigns menu. The scenario works best if you play it on moderate or harder.

The Tai Gun are a fictional Chinese tribe who started from humble beginnings as fishermen in the swamps. After years of hard work and successful trading, the influence of the Tai Gun has grown, and new settlements have been founded across the country. This has unfortunately raised the suspicion of the mighty Shang rulers, who do not tolerate anyone who becomes too influential. After several small attacks, the Shang have now sent the Tai Gun a clear message: "From the mud you came, and in the mud you will die."

Can you save the Tai Gun from annihilation, or will your failure be the reason they never made it into the history books? Defend your village in alligator-infested swamps, outsmart assassins in a luxurious temple park, infiltrate an enemy town for a dangerous prison escape mission, pray that the gods accept a sacrifice in the hour of need, face the Shang's most feared general and finally encounter the Shang Emperor himself.

------------------------------------------------------

I first published The Tai Gun for RoR in 1998. This 20th anniversary version has been updated to run with AoE:DE, and includes some new map design features (e.g. a new type of hedges, rocky bridges, overlapping buildings etc.). This campaign is designed to be tough, but winnable. If you are good at micromanagement, if you like fixed force mixed with a few build & destroy elements, this may be the right campaign for you. 

Enjoy, save often, and leave a Like!

modName: Laser War - Ruined mode (Up to 8 players)
description:
Laser War - Ruined mode

A fun game mode where you control 10 Photon Man on an almost symmetrical map. To win you must hunt down your enemy and be the last man standing. Or control all 5 ruines. Supports up to 8 players.


History:

Back in the days you could not place cheat units in the editor. So what people did was activate cheats and ally each other in the lobby. Once the game launched, everybody would type 10x Photon Man. Once ready and people cross checked each other's units they would delete all villagers and Town Centres. _(This way nobody can sneak in more photon man and "cheat")_

As you can see, this requires some trust among all players and setting up such games requires quite some effort. To avoid stressful situations for the players, I've made this map. Now you can play this fun mode more easily with total strangers.

Give it a go!

If you have any concerns, questions or feedback: PM me on the forum or visit the thread which I created for this game mode on the Definitive Edition section.

Cheers,

Helepolis

Hey guys, it's been nearly 20 years since I released a custom campaign for Age of Empires/Rise of Rome! But after working on the revamped campaigns for the Definitive Edition, I had to put something together for the custom campaign community. I hope you try it and enjoy it. Let me know what you think! 

About the Scenario
Sometime in the 6th century BCE, a Carthaginian crew passed through the Pillars of Herakles and down the West African coast. Led by a man named Hanno, the expedition would travel further than any Carthaginians had gone before. Their feat would not be matched until the Portuguese explorers some two millennia later. As Hanno, take charge of this voyage, fight savage tribes, and return to Carthage laden with the riches of Africa!

About the Author
The author is a campaign designer on the Forgotten Empires team, where he made official campaigns for both Age of Empires: Definitive Edition and Age of Empires II: HD Edition. He is also a prolific custom campaign designer with over a dozen releases totaling over 146,000 unique downloads.  Hanno and the African Voyage is his first released custom campaign for Age of Empires/Rise of Rome since 1999.

A Greek force of approximately 7,000 men marched north to block the pass in the summer of 480 BC. The Persian army, alleged by the ancient sources to have numbered over one million but today considered to have been much smaller (various figures are given by scholars ranging between about 100,000 and 150,000), arrived at the pass in late August or early September. The vastly outnumbered Greeks held ### ### ######## for seven days (including three of battle) before the rear-guard was annihilated in one of history’s most famous last stands. During two full days of battle the small force led by King Leonidas I of Sparta blocked the only road by which the massive Persian army could pass. After the second day of battle a local resident named Ephialtes betrayed the Greeks by revealing a small path that led behind the Greek lines. Leonidas, aware that his force was being outflanked, dismissed the bulk of the Greek army and remained to guard the rear with 300 Spartans, 700 Thespians, 400 Thebans and perhaps a few hundred others, most of whom were killed. The island of Rhodes was a mercantile republic with a large navy which controlled the entrance to the Aegean Sea. Rhodes maintained treaties of neutrality with other empires to protect trade. However, they had a close relationship with Ptolemy I and Demetrius was worried Rhodes would supply him with ships. Demetrius also saw the possibility of using Rhodes as a base. The decision to lay siege to Rhodes was influenced by these fears but it was also a piratical enterprise by Demetrius. Much of the Greek and Macedonian world, regardless of whether they were allies of Demetrius or not, apparently also viewed the siege as a pirate attack and sympathized with the Rhodians. As well as a fighting fleet of 200 ships and 150 auxiliary vessels Demetrius also enlisted the aid of many pirate fleets. Over 1,000 private trading vessels followed his fleets in anticipation of the plunder success would bring.

Siege
The city and main harbour of Rhodes was strongly fortified and Demetrius was unable to prevent supply ships from running his blockade so capturing the harbour was his main priority. He first built his own harbour alongside and constructed a mole from which he deployed a floating spiked boom but Demetrius never succeeded in taking the harbour. At the same time his army ravaged the island and built a huge camp next to the city but just out of missile range. Early in the siege the walls were breached and a number of troops entered the city but they were all killed and Demetrius didn’t press the attack. The walls were subsequently repaired.

Both sides used many technical devices during the siege such as mines and countermines and various siege engines. Demetrius even built the now notable siege tower, known as the Helepolis, in his attempt to take the city.

The citizens of Rhodes were successful in resisting Demetrius; after one year he abandoned the siege and signed a peace agreement (304 BC) which Demetrius presented as a victory because Rhodes agreed to remain neutral in his war with Ptolemy (Egypt). The unpopularity of the siege may have been a factor in its abandonment after only one year.

Several years later the Helepolis, which had been abandoned, had its metal plating melted down and, along with the money from selling the remains of the siege engines and equipment left behind by Demetrius, was used to erect a statue of their sun god, Helios, now known as the Colossus of Rhodes, to commemorate their heroic resistance.

The Third Punic War (149–146 BC) involved an extended siege of Carthage, ending in the city's thorough destruction. The resurgence of the struggle can be explained by growing anti-Roman agitations in Hispania and Greece, and the visible improvement of Carthaginian wealth and martial power in the fifty years since the Second War.

With no military, Carthage suffered raids from its neighbor Numidia. Under the terms of the treaty with Rome, such disputes were arbitrated by the Roman Senate. Because Numidia was a favored client state of Rome, Roman rulings were slanted heavily in favor of the Numidians. After some fifty years of this condition, Carthage had managed to discharge its war indemnity to Rome, and considered itself no longer bound by the restrictions of the treaty, although Rome believed otherwise. modName: Indigenous Canaan Map and Mideast Roman-Parthian Wars Map
description:
Maps:

  • "Land of Canaan Map with Indigenous Powers"
  • "Middle East (North) Map with Roman-Parthian Wars"

Features:

  • Single and Multiplayer
  • Detailed Instruction and History Screens
  • Players start in their historical locations with food resources nearby
  • Balanced so that you can choose any Player in Single or Multiplayer

INSTALLATION: Put the Mod's Scenario files into the "Scenarios" Folder of your User Directoru's AOE:DE folder.
The Default path if you have a Steam Account is:
C:\Users\_(Your_Username)\Games\Age of Empires DE\Users\(Your_Steam_Username)\Game Content\Scenarios

You are welcome to use these maps to make your own scenarios.

SCENARIO #1: "Land of Canaan Map with Indigenous Powers"

Number of Players: Up to 6 Human Players (3 v. 3 Map)
Difficulty: Easy-Medium
Acknowledgements: Mysterious guy, designer of the geographical terrain.


TEAM 1: Player 1: Israel (Phoenician Civ), Player 2: Phoenicia, Player 4: Egypt
TEAM 2: Player 3: Philistines (Minoan Civ), Player 5: Assyria, Player 7: Canaanites (Phoenician Civ)

Player 6 is inactive, must be the computer, and is just a war chest Ark that Israel gets automatically.


OBJECTIVE: Achieve a Standard Victory. Destroy the enemy forces, capture and hold the Ruins, or build a Wonder. The Ark is not a Victory Condition.


MYSTERIOUS GUY'S INTRODUCTION:

Battle or achieve superiority in one of the most history charged regions of the planet, where hundreds of kingdoms and civilizations rose and fell, a buffer zone between the Egyptian and the Hittite empire, a gateway for the Assyrians and the Babylonians to get to Egypt. Combat and ambush your foes in the central woody hills, settle in the rich Galilee or along the fertile Jordan valley, seek refuge in the scarped steppes of the Negev desert.

The land included in the scenario is what nowdays would be Israel, The Territories, the east bank of the Jordan river, a part of southern Lebanon and a small part of Syria.

 

################# OVERVIEW:

The scenario is a competition between 6 powers native to the region. The Israelites have defeated the Jebusites and made Jerusalem their capitol. They hold the Ark of the Covenant. The Canaanites remain in the region near Hazor. The Israelites, Egyptians, and Phoenicians are allied to each other, whereas the Canaanites, Assyrians, and Philistines are allied to each other on another Team.

HISTORY:

  • Land of Canaan, 1050 BC - 700 BC

The Israelites' capitol was Jerusalem, which they conquered from the Canaanite Jebusites in c. 1000 BC. According to the Biblical account, the Israelites' forefather Abraham was not a Canaanite but came from the city of Haran in Aramea, located in what is today southeast Turkey. Genesis 24 narrates that Abraham even made his servant promise to get wives for Abraham's sons from Abraham's land instead of from the Canaanites. The Bible narrates some Israelites coming from the Canaanites, and theories differ on how many Israelites came from them. Before about 1050 BC, the Israelites, Canaanites, and Phoenicians all shared about the same language and alphabet.

The Israelites conquered the largest Canaanite city, Hazor, in about the 14th-13th centuries B.C., but Canaanites remained in the region. The Canaanites shared the ethnicity of the Phoenicians.

The Phoenicians' leading cities included Tyre and Sidon. They were a seafaring people famous for their Crimson or Purple dyes. In fact, "Phoenicians" was the Greek name for this people, and it likely referred to their Purple or Crimson dye. Phoenician soldiers' clothes bore this color. The Israelites allied with Phoenicia, and in the Biblical story, the Phoenicians sent supplies for the building of Solomon's Temple.

Although the Israelites' famous Exodus with the Ark of the Covenant was a flight from Egypt, they eventually allied with Egypt. The Egyptians had a strong connection to the Sinai region. The Sinai city of Rafah was part of the Egyptian Empire since the times of the Pharaohs.

Assyria's original main cities included Ashur and Nineveh in what is today northern Iraq. Although the region of "Syria" got its name due to its conquest by Assyria, they are diferent regions: Assyria was in Mesopotamia, whereas Syria lies to its west and includes the city of Damascus. The Assyrian Empire became powerful through its use of iron, spread across the Middle East. In the 8th century BC, Assyria conquered Damascus and the Northern Kingdom of Israel, and subjugated the Southern Kingdom of Judah.

 

 

SCENARIO #2: "Middle East (North) Map with Roman-Parthian Wars"

Number of Players: 6-8 Human Players (3 v. 3 v. 2 Map, with Team 3 better as Passive AIs)
Difficulty: Medium-Hard
Acknowledgements: S_Bishop, designer of the geographical terrain

TEAM 1: Player 1. Parthians (Persian), P.6. Mesopotamia (Babylonian), and P.8. Media (Persian)
TEAM 2: Player 2. Roman Syria (Roman), P.5. Roman Asia Minor (Roman), and P.7. Roman Egypt (Roman)
TEAM 3: Player 3. Passive Armenia (Greek AI) and P.4. Osroene (Palmyran AI)

OBJECTIVE: Achieve a Standard Victory. Destroy the enemy forces, capture and hold the Ruins, gather all Artifacts, or build a Wonder. 

S_BISHOP'S INSTRUCTIONS: Walk in the footsteps of Alexander the Great and build an empire reaching from the eastern Mediterranean to the far reaches of Persia.

 

################# OVERVIEW: 

This scenario features the participants of the Roman - Parthian Wars: the Roman Empire, the Parthian Empire and its Median allies, and the "buffer state" kingdoms of Armenia and Osroene. The Roman-Parthian Wars were a regional conflict that lasted centuries, following the decline of the kingdoms of the Greek heirs of Alexander the Great. The Players all begin in the Tool Age. The Scenario was designed in AOE:ROR format, but tailored and playtested for AOE:DE.

HISTORY:

  • Northern Middle East, Late 1st Century BC - Early 7th Century AD

Alexander the Great conquered Persia in the mid 4th century BC, installing Greek Seleucid rulers in his wake. A century later, the Persian Parni tribe arose and took power in the region of Parthia. Their leader ######## I founded the Parthian Empire, and by 200 BC his dynasty made Saddarvazeh ("Hecatompylos" in Greek) its capitol. However, Tysfwn ("Ctesiphon" in Greek) near ancient Babylon became the most important Persian city in the 2nd-1st centuries BC. Meanwhile, the Medes, whose regional capitol was Praaspa, served as important Persianallies.

Alexander the Great's legacy of European political power in the Middle East was claimed by the Roman empire, which crushed the Seleucid army of Antiochus III the Great at the Battle of Magnesia and annexed Asia Minor in the Treaty of Apamea in 190 and 188 BC, respectively. In the wake of these events, the Roman Empire and the Parthian Empire became the two most powerful empires in the Middle East.

Armenia and its ally Osroene served as a buffer between the two empires, at times holding independence and other times shifting allegiance between them. During the reign of Tigranes the Great in 95–55 BC, Armenia was quite powerful. It raided the Persian city of Ecbatana and annexed the Seleucid city of Antioch, Syria, in 87 and 83 BC, respectively. However, the Roman general Lucullus defeated the Armenian king Tigranes at the Battle of Tigranocerta. In the wake of the Armenian defeat, the residents of Antioch hailed as their king a Seleucid, Antiochus III, whomLucullus appointed as the Romans' client ruler of Syria in 69 BC. At same year, the Roman commander ######### defeated Tigranes' ally, Abgar I. Abgar was the king of Osroene, whose capitol was Edessa.

These events led to major Roman expansion in the Mideast, and thus to competition over control of Armenia. The Roman ruler Pompey made the Kingdom of Armenia a Roman protectorate under the Armenian Artaxiad dynasty in 66-65 BC. The Romans took Antioch from the Seleucids in 64 BC and Egypt, whose capitol was Alexandria, from Queen Cleopatra in 30 BC.

The Roman emperor Augustus installed Tigranes V as king of Armenia in 6 AD, and Tigranes V shared power with Erato of Armenia. Then in 35 AD, the Roman emperor Tiberius invaded Armenia and installed Mithridates of Armenia as his client king. The Parthians subjugated Armenia in 37 AD, but the Romans retook control in 47 AD.

Rhadamistus, from the Kingdom of Iberia (what is today Georgia in the Caucasus) invaded, killed Mithridates, and made himself king of Armenia in 51 AD. In response, the Parthian king Vologases I invaded and placed his brother Tiridates I on the Armenian throne the next year. But the Parthian forces withdrew due to an epidemic, so Rhadamistus returned and punished the local populace. A local revolt put Tiridates I back on the Armenian throne in 55 AD. How would the Romans react?

Embark on an epic quest spanning 2000 years, leading the world's greatest empires of the Fertile Crescent, the Levant and the Near East. Take the reins of Sumeria, Egypt, Babylon, Assyria, the Hittite Empire, Israel, Phoenicia, the Kingdom of Lydia, the Persian Empire, the Kingdom of Saba in modern day Yemen and Palmyra and recreate the legacy of these ancient civilizations.

This campaign is a port of an old Rise of Rome campaign I had designed back in 2011, and I decided to meticulously update it with the DE version to take advantage of the improved eye candy and game mechanics. 

Don't forget to read the hints ~ I hope you enjoy the campaign! And please rate and review!

modName: Bow and Saddle - A Nomadic Saga
description:
This is a seven-parts campaign about the exploits and raids of various nomadic nations throughout History, from the late Bronze Age until the Fall of the Western Roman Empire.

Lead the mysterious Hyksos on their conquest of Egypt, the notorious Sea Peoples who contributed to the Bronze Age Collapse with their raids in the Eastern Mediterranean Sea, the Cimmerians and the Scythians on their looting of Anatolia, the birth of the mighty Parthian Empire from humble herders to conquerors of the Seleucid Empire, the fight of the Sarmatians against Roman expansion, and finally, lead the forces of Attila the Hun to the destruction of the ancient world.

This is mostly a Build and Destroy campaign and a couple of Fixed Force scenarios with various levels of difficulty and challenge mostly on real-world geography designed maps. Enjoy your pillaging and conquests!

modName: Quisque Barbarus Alio Est - Rome Versus the Barbarians
description:
Throughout their long history, the Roman people faced the threat of various northern European tribes that migrated and invaded the southern warmer and more fertile climates of Italy. The Celts and the Germans were the main adversaries of Rome across the ages. Sometimes the "barbarians" were victorious, other times Rome had the upper hand and ended up conquering those proud nations.

In this campaign you will witness the Sack of Rome by the Gauls after the Battle of Allia, the first Germanic incursions in Italy, the Gallic Wars fought by Julius Caesar, the defeat of Rome at the hands of Arminius and Boudicca, the Crisis of the Third Century and the Barbarian Invasions of the 5th century. 

Don't forget to always read the Hints section as some Victory and Loss Conditions are stated there. Enjoy your conquests!

{Edit: the Campaign title is an homage to the motto of the Rome Total War Europa Barbarorum mod ~ a great work of art}

modName: Dawn of Hellas - A Greek Epic
description:
This is the first of a two-parts series about the history of Ancient Greece, from 3000 BC until 479 BC. You will witness the rise of the Bronze Age cultures of the Cyclades, Minoan Crete and Mycenae, the fabled Trojan War, the Greek Dark Ages, the early conquests of Sparta, the civil strife inside Athens before the establishment of Democracy and the bloody Persian Wars.

Lead the Greeks to Glory, either through peaceful development and statecraft or through war and heroic struggle. Fortune Favours the Bold!

modName: The Whole Earth is the Sepulchre of Great Men
description:
After the end of the Persian Wars, the cities of Greece regained their strength and strived to become the leading forces of culture and wealth in the Eastern Mediterranean. Trade flourished in the Aegean, great works of art were created and the city states of Athens and Sparta vied for power and control of the Hellenic world. Under the guidance of prominent men like Cimon, Pericles, Lysander, Agesilaus and others, the Greeks ascended to greatness, but inevitably, due to their hubris and imperial ambitions, descended to bitter infighting and civil strife. Other illustrious men needed to appear, from the north and the Kingdom of Macedon, Philip II and his son Alexander, to unite the Greeks and launch them to their apogee with the invasion and conquest of the Persian Empire, that brought Hellenic culture to the ends of the earth.

In this continuation of the 'Dawn of Hellas' campaign, witness the Golden Age of Pericles and the formation of the Delian League that Athens used to expand its influence and maritime power in the Hellenic world, the Peloponnesian War and the rest of the civil conflicts that ultimately gave Macedon the opportunity to exert its might over the rest of Greece after the military triumphs of Philip II, and the epic campaign of Alexander the Great, who thanks to his strategic brilliance, defeated Darius, brought the whole of the Persian Empire to its knees, and led the Greeks from Europe to the icy peaks of the Hindu-Kush and the lush rainforests of India.

Expect action-packed missions with skirmishes on land and sea spanning across the Greek world and beyond, Build-and-Destroy scenarios along with pitched battles of Alexander against his many opponents -barbarian tribes, treacherous Greeks, Darius and the might of Persia, Scythian nomads, Porus and his Indian elephants- and many hours of conquest. Don't forget to always read the Hints section as some Victory and Loss Conditions are contained therein.

Enjoy the perilous world of Ancient Greece!

~ The campaign title is taken from a passage of Pericles' Funeral Oration from 'The History of the Peloponnesian War' (Book 2.34-46), written by Thucydides ~

It is the 16th century and Western European warfare as it was once known is changing. The houses of Habsburg and Valois have been at war for decades and they are using all their wealth and power to better each other. Infantry as a concept is going through a revolution with the return of the pike and the addition of the arquebus. Cannons deafen the battlefield with their roar. Light and Heavy Cavalry, although waning in power, still has its place in the battlefield and can turn the tide when wielded properly.

Welcome to a unique 4v4 PvP scenario where historical realism is represented in a fun and engaging struggle for roughly 30-45 minutes!

Players 5, 6, 7, 8 are the West Team. Players 1, 2, 3, 4 are the East Team.

Setting the map to explored is advised

Your main objective is to gather 1000 Victory Points for your team to win. The amount of Victory Points both teams have accumulated will be displayed on the top right of your screen. There are three ways to get victory points:

  • During each engagement there is a kill-to-death unit score. 
    • Both Teams start with a unit score of 0.
    • Each time a Western unit dies, 1 is added to the Eastern team unit score and 1 is subtracted from the Western team unit score.
    • Each time an Eastern unit dies, 1 is added to the Western team unit score and 1 is subtracted from the Eastern team unit score.
    • Whichever team reaches a unit score of 10 will gain 30 Victory Points, and then the unit scores are reset to 0, and then it all loops again.
  • Each player starts with at least one Captain. 
    • As long as a player has at least one Captain still alive, that player will earn 1 Victory Point every 15 seconds for his team.
    • A player can train as many Captains as he wants, but they cost food, pop space and are only useful on the battlefield for their long line of sight (20).
    • If all Captains of all players on one team die, then that team will lose the game.
  • There are three Monuments on the map. Each Monument will give 1 Victory Point every 15 seconds to the team that controls it.

You can only train one large army with the resources you start with (10,000 food, 10,000 gold, 300 wood, 5 stone). This is to historically simulate how a field battle begins. Resources will trickle slowly over time, and a mid-battle boost of resources will happen at 15 minutes, but your starting army is your main army for the most part.
